title: The Ultimate Guide to Astrophotography Locations Across the US
excerpt: From Death Valley to Acadia — the best dark sky sites in every region, with safety tips, Bortle ratings, and what to shoot at each.
author: Ace Stellar
body:
## Your Next Great Shot Is Somewhere Out There

The United States is quietly one of the best countries on Earth for astrophotography. We've got Bortle 1 skies in the desert Southwest, volcanic landscapes in Hawaii, aurora-lit wilderness in Alaska, and everything in between. From the ancient ruins of Chaco Canyon to the sea stacks of Olympic National Park, the foreground possibilities are endless and the skies are genuinely dark — if you know where to go.

I've spent years chasing dark skies across the US, and this guide is the result. Every location here has been chosen for a combination of sky darkness, stunning foregrounds, accessibility, and that hard-to-define "you have to see this" quality. I'll also be real with you about the dangers, because astrophotography means being alone in wild places in the dark. That's part of the magic, but it demands respect.

Let's start with the most important section.

## Safety First

I'm putting this at the top for a reason. Astrophotography takes you to remote, wild places in the middle of the night. That's inherently risky. The photos aren't worth your life or a trip to the ER. Read this section even if you think you already know it.

### Wildlife Encounters

The animals you'll share these locations with are not decorations. They're wild, and some are genuinely dangerous.

- **Bears (black and grizzly)** — Present throughout the Mountain West, Pacific Northwest, Northeast, and parts of the Southeast. Make noise while hiking in. Carry bear spray and know how to use it. Never store food near your gear. If you encounter a grizzly, do NOT run. Back away slowly, speak calmly, and make yourself look large. Black bears are generally more timid, but don't test them.
- **Mountain lions** — Common in the Southwest, California, Colorado, and other western states. They're ambush predators and most active at dawn and dusk, but they do move at night. Hike with a buddy when possible. If you see one, maintain eye contact, make yourself look big, and back away slowly. Never crouch or run.
- **Rattlesnakes** — Found across the Southwest, Great Plains, and Southeast. They're active at night during warm months, which is exactly when you're out shooting. Watch where you step and where you set your tripod. Use a headlamp on the ground ahead of you. Wear boots, not sandals.
- **Scorpions** — Common in the desert Southwest (Arizona, New Mexico, West Texas, Southern California). They glow under UV/blacklight, so carry a small UV flashlight to scan around your setup area. Their stings are painful and occasionally dangerous.
- **Alligators** — A real concern in Florida, Louisiana, and coastal Southeast. They're most active at night and are attracted to water edges. Don't set up right at the waterline in alligator country.
- **Mosquitoes and ticks** — Not life-threatening in the moment, but they carry Lyme disease, West Nile, and other nasties. Use DEET or picaridin-based repellent. Tuck pants into socks in tick country. Do a full tick check when you get home.

### Weather Hazards

- **Flash floods in desert environments** — This is the one that kills experienced outdoors people. A storm 20 miles away can send a wall of water down a dry wash with zero warning. NEVER camp or set up in a wash, slot canyon, or dry riverbed during monsoon season (July-September in the Southwest). Check weather radar obsessively.
- **Hypothermia at altitude** — Locations at 8,000+ feet (Grand Canyon North Rim, Great Sand Dunes, Mauna Kea) can drop 30-40 degrees after sunset, even in summer. A 70-degree afternoon becomes a 35-degree night. Bring serious layers: base layer, insulating mid-layer, windproof outer shell. Cotton kills — wear synthetics or wool.
- **Lightning** — Exposed ridgelines and mountain peaks are lightning magnets. If you can hear thunder, you're within strike range. Get off high ground immediately. Do NOT shelter under lone trees. Crouch low if you're caught in the open. Your tripod is a metal rod pointing at the sky — be aware.
- **Extreme heat** — Death Valley regularly exceeds 120 degrees F in summer and has hit 130+. Joshua Tree, Big Bend, and other desert locations can be dangerously hot May through September. Carry way more water than you think you need. At minimum one gallon per person per day, plus extra in your car.

### Remote Location Checklist

- **Tell someone your exact plan** — Where you're going, when you're leaving, when you expect to return. If you don't check in, they should call for help.
- **Check cell service beforehand** — Many of the best locations in this guide have zero cell service. Download offline maps (Google Maps, Gaia GPS, or AllTrails) before you leave.
- **Carry a first aid kit** — Include bandages, antiseptic, snake bite supplies (a pressure bandage and knowledge that you need to get to a hospital — NOT a tourniquet or suction kit), pain relievers, and any personal medications.
- **Bring more water and food than you need** — Your car might not start. The road might wash out. Plan for an unplanned overnight.
- **Carry a satellite communicator** — A Garmin inReach or SPOT device can save your life when there's no cell signal. They're $300-400 and worth every penny.
- **Full tank of gas** — Many of these locations are 50-100 miles from the nearest gas station. Fill up before the last stretch.

### Human Safety

- **Avoid areas with known crime issues** — Some remote desert areas near border crossings can be sketchy. Research current conditions. The BLM and NPS websites often have advisories.
- **Don't advertise expensive gear** — A camera, lenses, and star tracker can easily total $5,000-10,000. Be discreet at trailheads. Lock gear in your trunk, not visible on the back seat.
- **Go with a friend when possible** — Solo astrophotography is meditative and wonderful, but a buddy makes everything safer. If you go solo, see the checklist above.
- **Stay on marked trails and roads** — Getting lost in the desert or mountains at night can become a survival situation fast. Know your route in and out.

### Gear Protection

- **Dew** — A major problem in humid locations (Florida, Pacific Northwest, Appalachians). Dew on your front lens element ruins shots and you might not notice for 30 minutes. Use a dew heater strap on your lens. A hand warmer rubber-banded to the lens barrel works in a pinch.
- **Sand and dust** — Desert wind will sandblast your gear. Keep lens caps on when not shooting. Bring a blower to clean sensors. Change lenses in your car with the doors shut, not in open wind.
- **Cold batteries** — Below 40 degrees F, lithium-ion batteries lose significant capacity. Keep spares in your inner jacket pocket, close to body heat. Swap cold batteries in and warm them up; they'll recover some charge.
- **Condensation** — Moving gear from cold outdoor air to a warm car causes instant condensation on and inside your lenses. Put your camera in a sealed plastic bag before bringing it inside. Let it equalize slowly.
- **Theft at trailheads** — Smash-and-grabs happen at remote trailheads, especially popular ones. Don't leave any gear visible in your vehicle. Take everything valuable with you or lock it in the trunk before arriving at the location (thieves watch for people stashing things in trunks at the trailhead).

OK, now that we've covered how to not die or lose your gear — let's talk about where to go.

## Southwest (Arizona, New Mexico, Utah, West Texas)

The American Southwest is astrophotography paradise. Consistently clear skies, low humidity, minimal light pollution, and some of the most dramatic foregrounds on the planet. If you're serious about night sky photography, you'll end up here eventually.

### Big Bend National Park, Texas

**Bortle: 1-2** | **Best months: October - March** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Big Bend is one of the darkest places in the lower 48 states. The nearest significant light source is hundreds of miles away in any direction, and it shows. The Milky Way here casts visible shadows on the ground. That's not an exaggeration.

The park offers incredible diversity of foregrounds: the Chisos Mountains, the Rio Grande winding through Santa Elena Canyon, vast desert flats, and the iconic "Window" rock formation. The Santa Elena Canyon is a jaw-dropping Milky Way composition in spring when the core rises directly above the canyon mouth.

**Watch out for:** Extreme heat (summer daytime temps regularly exceed 110 degrees F), rattlesnakes (very common, especially near water), and the sheer remoteness. The nearest full-service town (Alpine) is 100+ miles from most of the park. Carry extra water, fuel, and spare tires. Cell service is essentially nonexistent.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Santa Elena Canyon, star trails from the Chisos Basin, the Window with a starfield, zodiacal light in spring and fall.

### Chaco Culture National Historical Park, New Mexico

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: April - October** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Chaco is an IDA-certified International Dark Sky Park, and it takes that designation seriously. The park rangers host regular astronomy programs and keep light pollution to an absolute minimum. The combination of 1,000-year-old Ancestral Puebloan ruins and pristine dark skies is genuinely spiritual.

Pueblo Bonito, the largest great house, makes a spectacular foreground under the Milky Way. The alignment of the buildings with astronomical events isn't a coincidence — these people knew the sky intimately.

**Watch out for:** The road in is notoriously rough — 20+ miles of washboard dirt/gravel that can take an hour. It's impassable when wet. Check road conditions before going (call the visitor center). There's no food, gas, or cell service in the park. Bring everything.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Pueblo Bonito, star trails over the great kiva at Casa Rinconada, Pueblo del Arroyo under starlight. The park occasionally offers special night access for photographers — check their website.

### Monument Valley, Utah/Arizona Border

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: March - October** | **Permit: Navajo Nation park fee; guide required for backcountry**

Those iconic mitten buttes under a star-filled sky? It's even better in person. Monument Valley offers foregrounds that are instantly recognizable and utterly dramatic. The red sandstone formations against the Milky Way create images that almost look fake because they're so perfect.

**Watch out for:** This is Navajo Nation tribal land. Respect the regulations. A Navajo Nation park permit is required, and you need a Navajo guide for any off-road or backcountry access (including many of the best photo spots). Several local guide services offer night photography tours — this is the way to go. Don't trespass. The Valley Drive road is rough and not suitable for low-clearance vehicles.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way behind the Mittens (spring alignment is perfect), star trails over Totem Pole, the Three Sisters at blue hour transitioning into night.

### Grand Canyon, North Rim, Arizona

**Bortle: 3** | **Best months: May - October (North Rim closed in winter)** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

The North Rim is significantly darker and less crowded than the South Rim. At 8,000+ feet elevation, you're above much of the atmospheric haze, and the canyon provides an epic foreground that needs no introduction. Bright Angel Point and Cape Royal are both outstanding night photography locations.

**Watch out for:** The altitude means cold nights — expect temperatures in the 30s and 40s even in summer. The North Rim is only open mid-May to mid-October (the road closes for winter). Mule deer and elk are common on the roads at night. Lightning storms roll through frequently in July/August monsoon season. Stay back from the rim in the dark — there are unfenced drop-offs measured in thousands of feet.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way arching over the canyon from Cape Royal, star trails from Bright Angel Point, airglow above the vast canyon at Point Imperial.

### Death Valley National Park, California/Nevada

**Bortle: 1** | **Best months: October - April** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Death Valley holds one of the darkest measured skies in the United States. The Racetrack Playa, Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes, and Zabriskie Point offer otherworldly foregrounds under a sky so dark you can see your own shadow by the light of the Milky Way.

This is a bucket list location for astrophotography, and for good reason.

**Watch out for:** EXTREME HEAT. Death Valley holds the record for the highest reliably recorded air temperature on Earth (134 degrees F). Summer temperatures regularly exceed 120 degrees. People die here every year from heat exposure. DO NOT attempt astrophotography May through September unless you're experienced in desert survival. Even October and March nights can still be hot. Carry a minimum of two gallons of water per person. The Racetrack Playa requires 27 miles of rough dirt road (high clearance vehicle, no RVs, check conditions). Cell service is nonexistent in most of the park.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the Racetrack Playa sailing stones, star trails from Zabriskie Point, Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes under starlight, the Milky Way from Dante's View overlooking the lowest point in North America.

## West Coast (California, Oregon, Washington)

The West Coast offers an incredible variety: alien rock formations, volcanic craters, old-growth rainforest, and rugged sea stacks. Light pollution from the major cities is real, but you don't have to drive far to escape it.

### Joshua Tree National Park, California

**Bortle: 3-4** | **Best months: October - April** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Joshua Tree's twisted trees and jumbled boulder formations create alien-looking foregrounds that pair beautifully with the night sky. The park is close enough to LA and San Diego for a weekend trip but dark enough for impressive results, especially if you head to the less-visited eastern half of the park (Bortle 2-3 near the Pinto Basin).

**Watch out for:** Rattlesnakes and scorpions — both are abundant and active at night during warm months. UV flashlight is your friend here. The temperature swing between day and night is dramatic in the desert: a 90-degree day can become a 45-degree night. Joshua Tree has become extremely popular — parking lots fill up, and other photographers' headlamps can ruin long exposures. Weeknight visits are far better than weekends.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over iconic Joshua trees (Skull Rock area, Hall of Horrors), star trails over Arch Rock, the Milky Way from Keys View looking over the Coachella Valley lights below.

### Crater Lake National Park, Oregon

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: July - September (winter road closures)** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

The deepest lake in the United States, sitting inside a collapsed volcano, reflecting a pristine dark sky. On a calm night, the Milky Way reflects perfectly in the impossibly blue water with Wizard Island in the foreground. It's one of the most stunning astrophotography compositions in the country.

**Watch out for:** Snow. Crater Lake gets an average of 43 feet of snow per year. The Rim Drive doesn't fully open until sometime in July most years, and sections can close again by early October. Even in summer, nights at 7,000+ feet are cold — bring winter layers in August. The road to the lake has steep drop-offs and no guardrails in many sections. Drive slowly at night.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way reflected in the lake with Wizard Island (the money shot — Watchman Overlook or Discovery Point), star trails over the caldera, Phantom Ship rock formation under stars.

### Olympic National Park, Washington

**Bortle: 2-3 (coastal areas)** | **Best months: June - September (clearest weather)** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Olympic's wild Pacific coast offers dramatic sea stacks silhouetted against the Milky Way. Rialto Beach and Ruby Beach are the marquee locations — massive rock pillars rising from the surf line under dark skies. The temperate rainforest interior (Hoh Rain Forest) adds a completely different feel.

**Watch out for:** Tides. This is critically important. Some beach areas become impassable at high tide, and you can get trapped against cliffs. Check tide tables before going and know your escape routes. The Pacific Northwest is famously cloudy — clear nights are precious and unpredictable. Be prepared to wait days for a clear window. Roosevelt elk are common on roads at night.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over sea stacks at Rialto Beach (iconic), Ruby Beach with sea stacks and driftwood, star trails from Second Beach, the Milky Way through gaps in the Hoh Rain Forest canopy.

### Trona Pinnacles, California

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: October - April** | **Permit: Free (BLM land)**

Over 500 tufa formations rising from a dry lakebed in the Mojave Desert. It looks like another planet, and it's been used as an alien landscape in dozens of movies and TV shows. Under a dark sky, it's surreal in the best way. And it's free.

**Watch out for:** Extremely remote — the nearest town (Trona) is tiny and has limited services. The access road is unpaved and can be rough. No facilities of any kind. No water, no restrooms, no cell service. This is pure backcountry. Bring everything. Abandoned mines dot the area — stay out of them. Summer temperatures are brutal.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way rising behind the pinnacles, light-painted tufa formations under stars, panoramic nightscapes across the entire pinnacle field, star trails with pinnacles in foreground.

## Mountain West (Colorado, Wyoming, Montana, Idaho)

Big mountains, big skies, big wildlife. The Mountain West has some of the most pristine dark skies remaining in the country, combined with world-class mountain scenery. Just remember that altitude, weather, and large predators demand extra preparation.

### Central Idaho Dark Sky Reserve

**Bortle: 1** | **Best months: June - October** | **Permit: Varies by location**

The largest dark sky reserve in the United States, covering over 1,400 square miles of central Idaho wilderness. The towns of Stanley and Ketchum serve as gateways to some of the darkest skies you'll ever experience. The Sawtooth Mountains provide jagged, dramatic foregrounds under skies where the zodiacal light, gegenschein, and airglow are all visible to the naked eye.

**Watch out for:** This is serious bear country — both black bears and the occasional grizzly. Carry bear spray. Wolves are present too (they won't bother you, but it's worth knowing). The area is remote and rugged. Mountain roads can be narrow and unpaved. Cell service is spotty to nonexistent outside of towns. Temperatures at 6,000-8,000 feet drop sharply at night.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the Sawtooth Mountains from Redfish Lake, star reflections in Stanley Lake, panoramic nightscapes from Galena Summit, the zodiacal light in spring and fall.

### Yellowstone and Grand Teton National Parks, Wyoming

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: June - September** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

The Tetons rising above Jackson Lake under the Milky Way is one of the most photographed nightscapes in the world, and for good reason — it's breathtaking. Yellowstone adds geothermal features (steaming geysers under stars!) to the mix.

**Watch out for:** Grizzly bears. This is not optional knowledge. Yellowstone and the Tetons have one of the densest grizzly populations in the lower 48. Carry bear spray (available at every park gift shop), know how to use it, and make noise while hiking in the dark. Bison are also dangerous — they can run 35 mph and will charge if you get too close. Yellowstone's thermal features are genuinely deadly — people have died falling into boiling springs. Stay on boardwalks, especially in the dark.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the Tetons from Schwabacher Landing or Oxbow Bend, Old Faithful erupting under stars (tricky timing but spectacular), Grand Prismatic Spring at blue hour transitioning to stars, star trails over Yellowstone Lake.

### Great Sand Dunes National Park, Colorado

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: March - October** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

The tallest sand dunes in North America (up to 750 feet) set against the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The dunes create sweeping, minimalist foregrounds under incredibly dark skies. The high altitude (8,200 feet at the dunefield, higher if you climb) puts you above most atmospheric haze.

**Watch out for:** Altitude — at 8,000+ feet, you'll feel it, especially if you're climbing the dunes. Take it slow. Night temperatures can drop below freezing even in summer. The sand retains heat during the day but cools rapidly after sunset. Medano Creek floods the flat area between the parking lot and the dunes during spring snowmelt (May-June) — you may need to wade through it. Lightning storms are common in July/August.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Star Dune (the tallest), rippled sand patterns in foreground with stars above, star trails over the dunes with mountain backdrop, the Sangre de Cristo range as foreground.

### Glacier National Park, Montana

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: July - September (Going-to-the-Sun Road open)** | **Permit: Park entrance fee + vehicle reservation in peak season**

Glacier's alpine scenery is world-class — jagged peaks, turquoise lakes, and remnant glaciers under dark Montana skies. Wild Goose Island on Saint Mary Lake under the Milky Way is an iconic composition.

**Watch out for:** Grizzly bears. Glacier has a high density of grizzlies, and unlike Yellowstone, the terrain here funnels you into narrow valleys where surprise encounters are more likely. Always carry bear spray. Hike in groups. Make noise. Going-to-the-Sun Road is one of the most spectacular drives in America, but it's narrow, steep, and has sheer drop-offs — drive it carefully, especially at night. Snow closes the road from October through June most years.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Wild Goose Island from Sun Point, stars reflected in Two Medicine Lake, the Milky Way from Logan Pass, star trails over Many Glacier Hotel.

## Great Plains & Midwest

People overlook the middle of the country for astrophotography, and they shouldn't. Some of the darkest skies in the lower 48 are in the Dakotas, Nebraska, and Kansas — and the wide-open horizons give you an unobstructed view from horizon to horizon that mountain locations can't match.

### Badlands National Park, South Dakota

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: April - October** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

The Badlands' eroded spires, pinnacles, and layered rock formations look alien even in daylight. Under a dark sky, they become unearthly. The formations catch starlight and shadow in ways that create dramatic, textured foregrounds unlike anything else.

**Watch out for:** Rattlesnakes are common in the park, especially in rocky areas where you'd be setting up tripods. Watch your step. The formations are fragile and have steep, crumbly drop-offs — don't climb in the dark. Temperature extremes are real: summer days can hit 100+ degrees, and winter nights can drop to -20 degrees F. Severe thunderstorms with lightning, hail, and tornadoes are possible May through August.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the Badlands Wall from the Door Trail or Pinnacles Overlook, star trails over the formations from the Ben Reifel area, the Milky Way panorama from Sage Creek Basin (even darker, Bortle 1-2).

### Theodore Roosevelt National Park, North Dakota

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: May - September** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

One of the least visited national parks in the system, which is a huge advantage for astrophotography. The painted canyons, bison herds, and wide-open grasslands under North Dakota's vast sky are seriously underrated. The South Unit is more accessible; the North Unit is darker and more remote.

**Watch out for:** Bison roam freely and can be aggressive. Give them a very wide berth, especially at night when they might be bedded down and you could stumble onto them. The park is remote — the nearest sizeable town (Dickinson or Williston) is 30-60 miles away. Winters are brutally cold (-30 degrees F is not unusual). Even fall nights can be bitter.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the painted canyons, bison silhouettes at twilight transitioning into stars, star trails from the Oxbow Overlook, the Milky Way over the Little Missouri River.

### Headlands International Dark Sky Park, Michigan

**Bortle: 3** | **Best months: April - October** | **Permit: Free**

Located at the tip of Michigan's Lower Peninsula near Mackinaw City, Headlands is the most accessible dark sky site for millions of people in the Great Lakes region. A short drive from Detroit, Chicago, or Milwaukee gets you to genuinely dark skies with a Lake Michigan foreground. The park was specifically designed for stargazing with covered observing pads and a dark sky discovery center.

**Watch out for:** Mosquitoes in summer are legendary. Bring serious repellent. Lake Michigan weather can shift rapidly — fog can roll in and end your session without warning. Bears are uncommon but present in the area. The park is small and can get crowded on clear weekend nights during events.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Lake Michigan with the Mackinac Bridge lights in the far distance, star trails over the lake, Northern Lights (this is one of the southern-most reliable aurora viewing locations in the eastern US during strong geomagnetic storms).

## Southeast

The Southeast gets a bad reputation for astrophotography due to humidity, light pollution, and tree cover, but there are genuine gems if you know where to look. The unique landscapes — swamps, barrier islands, mountain ridges — offer foregrounds you can't find anywhere else.

### Big Cypress National Preserve, Florida

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: November - April (dry season)** | **Permit: Free**

Adjacent to the Everglades, Big Cypress offers some of the darkest skies in the eastern United States. Cypress trees draped in Spanish moss standing in still water, reflecting the Milky Way — it's a look that's uniquely Southern and absolutely stunning.

**Watch out for:** Alligators. They are everywhere, especially near water. Do not wade into water in the dark. Stick to boardwalks and elevated areas. Mosquitoes during the wet season (May-October) are genuinely unbearable — not annoying, unbearable. Dry season (November-April) is the only practical window. Snakes (including water moccasins/cottonmouths and Eastern diamondback rattlesnakes) are common. Humidity will destroy your shots with dew — a dew heater is mandatory, not optional.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way reflected in still swamp water through cypress trees, star trails over the sawgrass prairie from Loop Road, the Milky Way from the Big Cypress Gallery area.

### Shenandoah National Park, Virginia

**Bortle: 4** | **Best months: April - October** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Not the darkest sky on this list, but incredibly accessible from the Washington DC metro area (90 minutes) and with beautiful Blue Ridge Mountain foregrounds. Skyline Drive provides dozens of overlooks at elevation, putting you above the valley haze. Big Meadows is the go-to location.

**Watch out for:** Black bears are common in the park — you'll likely see one during a multi-day visit. They're generally timid but store food properly and give them space. The Appalachian humidity means dew is almost guaranteed on your lens — bring a dew heater. Fog can appear instantly. The overlooks along Skyline Drive have stone walls but no railings in many places — watch your footing in the dark.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way rising over the Shenandoah Valley from Big Meadows, star trails from Stony Man summit, the Milky Way over fog-filled valleys (a stunning effect when conditions are right).

### Outer Banks, North Carolina

**Bortle: 3-4** | **Best months: March - October** | **Permit: Free (Cape Hatteras National Seashore)**

The Outer Banks' barrier islands stretch far enough from the mainland to escape most light pollution. The Cape Hatteras Lighthouse is an iconic American landmark that makes for an instantly recognizable astrophotography foreground. The wide, flat beaches offer unobstructed horizon views.

**Watch out for:** The ocean. Seriously. Strong currents and rip tides are constant. Don't wade in at night. Mosquitoes are fierce in summer. Wild horses on the northern islands (Corolla) can be aggressive — keep your distance. Hurricane season (June-November) can bring sudden severe weather. Sand will find its way into your gear — protect your camera bag and change lenses in your car.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Cape Hatteras Lighthouse (spiral stripes + Milky Way = chef's kiss), star trails from the beach at Jockey's Ridge, the Milky Way from Cape Lookout National Seashore (even darker, accessible only by ferry).

## Northeast

The densely populated Northeast seems like an impossible place for astrophotography, but pockets of genuine darkness exist. You just have to drive a bit farther to find them, and the locations that remain dark are protected and exceptional.

### Cherry Springs State Park, Pennsylvania

**Bortle: 2** | **Best months: April - October** | **Permit: Free**

The gold standard for dark sky sites on the East Coast. Cherry Springs is an IDA-certified Dark Sky Park surrounded by the Susquehannock State Forest, which acts as a buffer against light pollution from all directions. The astronomy observation field is specifically maintained for stargazing — red lights only, no white lights, serious protocols enforced.

**Watch out for:** It's a popular destination for astronomers, so the observation field can be crowded on clear weekend nights. Other people's red headlamps and screens can be distracting for wide-field photography. Arrive early to claim a good spot. Black bears are present in the surrounding forest. Rattlesnakes (timber rattlers) inhabit the rocky areas. Summer nights can be surprisingly cold at 2,300 feet.

**What to shoot:** Deep Milky Way panoramas (this is one of the few East Coast sites dark enough for full-arc panoramas), zodiacal light in spring and fall, widefield shots of the summer triangle, Andromeda Galaxy with just a telephoto lens.

### Acadia National Park, Maine

**Bortle: 3-4** | **Best months: May - October** | **Permit: Park entrance fee + vehicle reservation in peak season**

Cadillac Mountain in Acadia is famously the first place in the US to see sunrise from October through March. It's also a fantastic astrophotography destination with a unique combination of ocean, rocky coastline, and mountain summit. Bass Harbor Head Lighthouse under the stars is one of the most photographed night scenes in the Northeast.

**Watch out for:** The Atlantic coast is cold, even in summer — bring serious layers for nighttime shooting. Fog is extremely common and can appear in minutes. The granite surfaces of Cadillac Mountain are slippery when wet. The park is very popular and crowded in July/August — consider shoulder season (May, September, October) for fewer people and better conditions.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over Bass Harbor Head Lighthouse (the classic shot), star trails from Cadillac Mountain summit, the Milky Way reflected in Jordan Pond with the Bubbles in the background, Schoodic Point with ocean spray and stars.

### Adirondack Park, New York

**Bortle: 2-3** | **Best months: May - October** | **Permit: Free (public land)**

The largest publicly protected area in the contiguous United States — 6 million acres of mountains, forests, and over 3,000 lakes. Much of the interior is genuinely dark, with Bortle 2 readings common in the High Peaks region. The combination of mountain lakes reflecting stars and rugged Adirondack terrain is outstanding.

**Watch out for:** Black bears are common — proper food storage is required (bear canisters in the High Peaks region). The terrain is rugged and trail conditions vary from maintained paths to bushwhack-only routes. Getting lost in the Adirondacks is a real possibility — carry a map, compass, and GPS. Cell service is unreliable in most of the park. Blackflies in May-June are genuinely horrendous.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way reflected in Heart Lake with Mount Marcy beyond, star trails from the summit of Cascade Mountain (one of the easier High Peaks), the Milky Way over Lake Placid from Whiteface Mountain, nightscapes from the shores of Saranac Lake.

## Hawaii & Alaska

The extremes. Hawaii offers the best astronomical seeing conditions on Earth (there's a reason the world's most advanced telescopes are up there). Alaska offers the northern lights. Both are bucket list destinations for astrophotographers.

### Mauna Kea, Hawaii

**Bortle: 1** | **Best months: Year-round (April - September for Milky Way core)** | **Permit: Free (reservation required for summit access)**

At 13,796 feet, Mauna Kea's summit is above 40% of the Earth's atmosphere and nearly all water vapor. The seeing conditions here are among the best on Earth. The world's premier astronomical observatories are here for a reason. The sky at the summit is unlike anything you've experienced at sea level — more stars than you thought existed, the Milky Way bright enough to read by.

**Watch out for:** Altitude sickness is a serious and potentially life-threatening concern. You're going from sea level to nearly 14,000 feet, often in a single drive. Symptoms include headache, nausea, dizziness, and in severe cases, pulmonary or cerebral edema. Spend at least 30 minutes acclimatizing at the Visitor Information Station (9,200 feet) before continuing to the summit. Do not go to the summit if you've been scuba diving in the past 24 hours. The summit is cold — temperatures routinely drop below freezing even in summer, with wind chill making it feel much colder. There is no oxygen tank or medical facility at the top. The access road is steep, unpaved above the VIS, and requires 4WD. Note: Mauna Kea is sacred to Native Hawaiians. Be respectful of the cultural significance of this place.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the observatory domes (iconic), star trails from the summit, the Milky Way rising above the cloud layer below you, sunset transitioning to an unreal starfield.

### Haleakala National Park, Maui, Hawaii

**Bortle: 1-2** | **Best months: Year-round** | **Permit: Park entrance fee + sunrise reservation (if staying through sunrise)**

Haleakala's summit crater at 10,023 feet is another world-class dark sky site, slightly lower and more accessible than Mauna Kea. The crater's alien, Mars-like landscape of volcanic cinder cones creates surreal foregrounds. Above the clouds, the sky is phenomenal.

**Watch out for:** Same altitude concerns as Mauna Kea, though less severe at 10,000 feet. Still expect cold temperatures (30s-40s F) and wind. The drive up is long with many switchbacks — not fun in the dark if you're not used to mountain driving. Sunrise is the big tourist draw, so if you're shooting all night, the summit area gets packed around 5-6 AM. Nene (Hawaiian geese) nest near the summit — don't disturb them, they're endangered.

**What to shoot:** Milky Way over the volcanic crater and cinder cones, star trails from the summit overlook, zodiacal light (excellent visibility above the cloud layer), the Milky Way over the sea of clouds below.

### Denali National Park, Alaska

**Bortle: 1** | **Best months: September - March (dark sky season)** | **Permit: Park entrance fee**

Denali (formerly Mount McKinley) at 20,310 feet is the highest peak in North America, and the park surrounding it offers some of the most remote wilderness on the continent. But here's the catch with Alaska — from late May through July, the sky never gets dark enough for astrophotography. The midnight sun is incredible to experience but terrible for star photography.

The flip side? From September through March, the dark skies are paired with the Northern Lights. Aurora borealis displays over Denali are among the most spectacular on Earth.

**Watch out for:** Cold. Alaska cold. January temperatures in Denali can drop to -40 degrees F (which, fun fact, is the same in both Fahrenheit and Celsius). Your camera batteries will die in minutes without protection. Your tripod legs can freeze to your bare skin. Dress for Arctic conditions: insulated boots, expedition-weight base layers, down parka, balaclava, heavy mittens. Grizzly bears are present in the park from May through October. Moose are surprisingly dangerous and common year-round — they're massive and unpredictable.

**What to shoot:** Aurora borealis over Denali (THE bucket list shot), star trails over the Alaska Range, the Milky Way from Wonder Lake (the classic Denali reflection shot), aurora reflected in glacial rivers.

## Pro Tips for Location Scouting

Before you pack the car and drive 10 hours, do your homework.

- **Check the Bortle rating before committing** — Use lightpollutionmap.info or the Ace Stellar Light Map to verify current light pollution levels. New development can change conditions over time. A location that was Bortle 2 five years ago might be Bortle 4 now if a housing development went in nearby.
- **Scout foregrounds during the day** — Drive to the location in daylight and walk the terrain. Identify compositions, find stable ground for your tripod, note hazards, and figure out your entrance and exit routes. Trying to find a composition in the dark at a location you've never visited is frustrating and often unproductive.
- **Check permit requirements** — Many national parks, tribal lands, and wilderness areas require permits, fees, or reservations. Some restrict night access or require guided tours. Check the official website or call the ranger station before your trip. Getting turned away at the gate after a long drive is a bad feeling.
- **Download offline maps** — Google Maps, Gaia GPS, and AllTrails all offer offline map downloads. Cell service at most of these locations is nonexistent. A paper topographic map and compass are smart backup.
- **Arrive before sunset** — Give yourself at least 30 minutes before dark to set up, compose, and get comfortable with your surroundings. Rushing to set up in the dark at an unfamiliar location leads to forgotten gear, poor compositions, and missed safety hazards.
- **Check the Moon phase and rise/set times** — A full Moon washes out the Milky Way. Plan your trips around new Moon (+/- 5 days). Or use the Moon intentionally as a landscape light source — a quarter Moon illuminating foreground with stars above can be gorgeous.
- **Monitor weather obsessively** — Clear Sky Chart, the NWS point forecast, and satellite imagery are your best friends. Clouds are the astrophotographer's nemesis. Don't drive 8 hours on a hope — wait for a confirmed clear forecast.
- **Join local astrophotography groups** — Facebook groups, Reddit communities (r/astrophotography, r/LandscapeAstro), and local astronomy clubs are gold mines for location-specific tips, current conditions, and sometimes group shoots where you can learn from experienced photographers.

## Get Out There

The best astrophotography location is one you'll actually visit. Start with the closest site to you on this list. Make a plan, check the conditions, pack smart, and go. Your first trip to a Bortle 2 site will change how you think about the night sky forever.

And when you get that shot — the one where the Milky Way is arching over an incredible landscape and you can't believe your camera captured it — you'll understand why we drive hundreds of miles and stay up all night in the cold.

It's worth it. Every time.

Clear skies out there.
